EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fatal accident occurred on December 17th at 715 Pm MST on S 15th St E in Idaho Falls near Sandy Downs.  It was suspected weather may have been a factor as low visibility in fog and icy roads may have occurred.    A car slid off the road and a second vehicle was attempting to get the vehicle out of the snow.  A third vehicle struck the sedon vehicle pushing it into the first vehicle and a woman was between the first and second vehicles and died from her injuries.   the victim was a 58 year old female from Eastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal accident occurred on December 17th at 715 Pm MST on S 15th St E in Idaho Falls near Sandy Downs.  It was suspected weather may have been a factor as low visibility in fog and icy roads may have occurred.    A car slid off the road and a second vehicle was attempting to get the vehicle out of the snow.  A third vehicle struck the sedon vehicle pushing it into the first vehicle and a woman was between the first and second vehicles and died from her injuries.   the victim was a 58 year old female from Eastern Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kootenai River Complex was formed on September 2. It consisted of the Eneas Peak, Katka, Russell Mountain, Scotch Creek, and Trout Fires. It was activity until October 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Suspected lightning started the Columbus Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbus Fire was detected on August 22 and active through October. It is located approximately 6 miles northeast of Murray, ID, and is within a mile of the Idaho/Montana state line. This fire was likely a holdover lightning start from the same storm that started the Bear Gulch Fire. It has burned 2298 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds gusting over 60 mph combined with over a foot of new snow caused blowing and severe drifting snow which shut down travel and caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grangeville and Cottonwood areas had received 15 to 17 inches of snow with off and on periods of blowing and drifting by the end of December 2021. Southerly winds ramped up by the beginning of the new year, and reached a climax from the late morning to the afternoon on the 3rd as sustained winds between 40 and 50 mph and gusts nearing 70 mph occurred. Initially severe drifting was reported on Whitebird Grade and vicinity, where a spotter reported 2 to 3 foot drifts over their country road by the morning of the 2nd. When the winds ramped up by 4 am on the 3rd, severe drifting up to 4 feet high shut down Idaho 64. By 10 am on the 3rd, the 50 to 60+ mph winds and severe drifting shut down US-95 between Cottonwood and Grangeville until 2 am on the 4th. Other impacts include: Idaho 162 and many other prairie roads were shut down due to drifting snow, spotty power outages, schools were closed, roads stayed icy and intense drifting snow with wind gusts to 61 mph were reported on top of Greer Grade on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third straight week of heavy snow and winds brought another round of significant snowfall and strong winds. None of the breaks were longer than half a day so it was all considered one event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bern COOP observer reported 13 inches of snow during the storm with 8 inches on the 5th.   The Bear Lake School District 33 was closed on both the 5th and the 6th due to the combination of heavy snowfall and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions caused by 40 plus mph wind gusts created a 40 vehicle crash on US Highway 20 in the east bound lane on January 7th.   It initially occurred when a semi crashed into a stopped vehicle that was stopped due to the low visibility.   Ambulances were involved in the pile up.  The location was in the Henry's Lake flats in between Idaho Highway 87 and Island Park.   Two police deputies were struck as well.   Only very minor injuries were reported in the pile up with no deaths.   The crash occurred at around 3 pm MST and US Highway 20 was closed for several hours as well as Idaho Highway 87 from Island Park to the Montana state line for several hours.   Both were re-opened on the 8th.  Snowfall was not heavy in the 1 to 3 inch range but the extremely gusty winds created the whiteout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions caused by 40 plus mph wind gusts created a 40 vehicle crash on US Highway 20 in the east bound lane on January 7th.   It initially occurred when a semi crashed into a stopped vehicle that was stopped due to the low visibility.   Ambulances were involved in the pile up.  The location was in the Henry's Lake flats in between Idaho Highway 87 and Island Park.   Two police deputies were struck as well.   Only very minor injuries were reported in the pile up with no deaths.   The crash occurred at around 3 pm MST and US Highway 20 was closed for several hours as well as Idaho Highway 87 from Island Park to the Montana state line for several hours.   Both were re-opened on the 8th.  Snowfall was not heavy in the 1 to 3 inch range but the extremely gusty winds created the whiteout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a two day heavy snow event that impacted the northern Idaho Panhandle in late February. The first round spread across northern Washington into north Idaho on February 27th. A heavier round of precipitation arrived that evening into the 28th. It created hazardous travel conditions due to slick, snow covered roads. The two day event increased avalanche danger due to heavy wet snow falling on top of a layer of lighter snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate at 0000 PST on February 28th and taper off in the late evening around 2100 PST. A total of 8 to 10 inches of snow fell overnight, February 27th to 28th, across the northern Idaho Panhandle in locations such as Sandpoint, Elmira, and Coolin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school and business closed due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow with strong winds to the northern Idaho Panhandle. Locations across the Coeur d'Alene area received overnight snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches and reached 7 to 12 inches by the morning of January 7th. On the morning of January 6th, a vehicle slid off the road due to slick road conditions along the Lake Coeur d'Alene roads. No known injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow with strong winds to the northern Idaho Panhandle. Locations across Bonner and Boundary County received overnight snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches and reached 12 to 19 inches by the morning of January 7th. For many, the snow melted throughout the day and re-froze overnight the leading to icy roads. No known collisions, but multiple power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving storm system, with a deep fetch of Pacific moisture, delivered widespread moderate to heavy snow to the Inland Northwest tonight into Thursday. The winter storm severely impact transportation for almost one week. During the event, numerous school districts cancelled school and business closed due to the hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across central Idaho Panhandle with an overnight, 12 hour accumulation of 5 to 10 inches in Viola and Moscow on January 6th. Light snow continued off and on throughout the day with an additional 3 inches in Moscow over the following 24 hours and ending around 0700PST on the 7th. There were reports of blowing and drifting snow on US Highway 95 on the 6th. The Latah County government closed buildings in Moscow due to the poor weather and driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought very heavy snow to north-central Idaho that triggered multiple impacts including treefall, avalanche, road closures, and hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall totals amounted to around 2 feet in the area which resulted in poor driving conditions and a few power outages. Pierce received a 3 day snowfall total of 20.6 inches through the 7th, which was the 7th highest snowfall during any time of the year since 1963.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought very heavy snow to north-central Idaho that triggered multiple impacts including treefall, avalanche, road closures, and hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2 feet of heavy snow fell across the region with the Idaho Department of Transportation stating that the snow rates were up to 3 inches per hour at times. US-12 between Kooskia and Powell was closed from 2 pm PST Jan. 6 through 4 pm Jan. 9 due to avalanche and treefall danger. It was estimated that 150 trees fell due to the weight of the snow along the highway. Also a large avalanche occurred at milepost 136. Power outages were reported east of Kooskia and also in the Elk City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought very heavy snow to north-central Idaho that triggered multiple impacts including treefall, avalanche, road closures, and hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche, more than 10 feet deep and up to 40 feet wide was triggered by heavy snow near milepost 136 on US-12. This along with treefall caused the highway to be closed for 3 days. The last time an avalanche crossed US-12 was in 2008. The date and time of the avalanche is a coarse estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought very heavy snow to north-central Idaho that triggered multiple impacts including treefall, avalanche, road closures, and hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow amounts during this event included 10 inches on the Camas Prairie, 12.5 inches in Orofino, 14.8 inches located west-northwest of Orofino, and 18.5 inches in Woodland. Multiple trees fell onto US-12 between Orofino and Kamiah which halted plowing operations and damaged power lines. Besides a plethora of power outages, there were area-wide school closures and delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very long period of extremely cold conditions for mid April and a continual push of Pacific  moisture led to heavy mountain snowfall with snow levels down to valley floors.  Interstate pile ups and a snow related fatal airplane crash all occuured during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds caused whiteout conditions on interstate 84  with a multiple vehicle accident influenced by the weather occurring just south of the Yale Road Exit at around 5:30 MDT.  RWIS station ITD 12 near the incident reported 0.24 mile visibility at 415 PM and wind gusts as high as 66 mph at 445 PM.   Wind gusts over 50 mph through 645 PM.   Nine people were transported to hospitals.   AS many as 12 semi tractor trailers were involved.  Interstate 86 then closed from 630 PM MDT until 115 AM MDT on the 12th.

The employee estimated tornado wind speed to be around 65-75 mph. After the event, they found fragile brush laying flat in one direction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system brought instability that allowed for severe thunderstorms to form in southwest Idaho.  A strong gust front brought roof damage to local businesses in Buhl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nunes Dairy Farm just southwest of Buhl, Idaho lost its roof due to strong winds from a thunderstorm. Nearby trampolines and swing sets were also damaged. A gust of 58 mph was measured at the Jerome airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the migrating upper level low, the moist boundary layer and cool air aloft lead to conditions prime for funnel formation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold core funnel was spotted near Lewiston Hill by several public sources. The location was estimated based on reports and pictures. It did not impact people or property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation of up to 2 inches in a 2 day period, combined with high snow levels and snowmelt, caused overland flooding, as well as stream flooding and debris flow issues across north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plugged culverts and heavy rainfall contributed to flooding on Main Street in Grangeville and also in Cottonwood. Rock slides and mud slides were reported along Highway 95 between Riggins and White Bird and also along Big Salmon Road east of Riggins. Rock slides, mud slides and fallen trees occurred on Highway 12 between Kooskia and Jerry Johnson Hot Springs. Water from Sevenmile Creek also flowed over Highway 162 at milepost 20. Debris flow caused the Lolo Creek bridge along the Clearwater and Idaho County line to wash out. The Clearwater River at Stites briefly spiked up to 8.86 feet in the minor flood category, but no major impacts were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening Low with strong jet support brought strong surface winds across Columbia Basin into the region. Winds were sustained in the high teens and mid 20s. Gusts ranged in the upper 40s to near 60. Power outages affected around 15000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Site near Athol Idaho reported a Southwest 59MPH gust at 1430LST. Winds were strong from 1330-1530LST with gusts in the range of 30 to 50 MPH across the zone. The winds caused downed power lines and outages affecting around 2400 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of an atmospheric River, in late February and early March, brought heavy rainfall to the Pacific Northwest. The heaviest precipitation was late February 28th through March 1st. The temperatures associated with this event were warm enough to produce rain (rather than snow), but this also led to snow melt. In the central Idaho Panhandle, this produced strong responses to the rivers of affected areas in the first week of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were locations across the central Idaho Panhandle that the Coeur d'Alene River reached action or minor flood stage: Cataldo and Enaville. Low laying areas, fields, and campgrounds saw minor flooding. The Coeur d'Alene River View Road, from Cataldo to Kingston, was closed due to water seeping over the road. Enaville had minor road flooding and closures as well. The flooding was initiated by heavy rainfall and snow melt. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were locations across the central Idaho Panhandle that the Coeur d'Alene River reached action or minor flood stage: Dudley, Medimont, and Cataldo. Low laying areas, fields, and campgrounds saw minor flooding. The Coeur d'Alene River View Road, from Cataldo to Kingston, was closed due to water seeping over the road. The flooding was initiated by heavy rainfall and snow melt. There were no injuries or fatalities.

The Palouse River reached action stage from mid-day March 3rd until March 4th. The flooding was initiated by heavy rainfall and snow melt. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the fire on August 24. It became the Kootenai Complex on September 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russell Mountain Fire was reported early afternoon on August 24, 2022. It is located within the Selkirk Mountain Range on the Bonners Ferry Ranger District of the Idaho Panhandle National Forests. It is approximately 10 miles southwest of Copeland Idaho. It has burned 4811 acres of timber and brush. The Kootenai River Complex consists of the Eneas Peak, Katka, Russell Mountain, Scotch Creek, and Trout Fires. They have combined to burn 25401 acres. Cost of the Complex is around 15 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Blackburn fire was started from suspected lightning and discovered on August 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blackburn Fire is located just north of the Heller Creek Campground near the headwaters of the St. Joe River. It was discovered on August 14. Several other fires were detected the same weekend, all from a single lightning storm days before. It has burned 360 acres of timber.

This allowed for monsoon moisture to move into the region along with strong southwest flow. Several days of isolated supercells occurred, bringing heavy rain and large hail to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized flooding occurred on State Highway 167 and State Highway 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell from a thunderstorm causing extensive nuisance street and parking lot flooding in the Idaho Falls area.   Generally 0.70 to just over an inch of rain fell in the 4 to 5 pm MDT period causing the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of rainfall in the 0.80 to just over an inch range from about 4 pm MDT to 5:30 pm MDT caused extensive nuisance street and parking lot flooding in the Idaho Falls area.   One foot of water was reported across the intersection of Druvor St and Lowell Dr.  Flooding was reported near Albertsons at the intersection of E 17th St and S Holmes Ave.  Many reports of street and parking lot flooding in the Ammon area.  The intersection of Austin Ave and 12 St was flooded.  Parking lot flooding up to 1 foot deep in the plaza including TJ Max.  Areas of water were 1 to 2 feet deep in the Grand Teton Mall parking lot and over the bottoms of some vehicles.  Ashment Ave flooded north of E 17th St and there was flooding in business parking lots along S 25th E including across the street from Target.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rain fell with several reports of 1 to 3 inches falling.   Flash flooding was widespread across southeasy Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred north of Roy due to an inch of rain.  Minor road damage on Lawery Lane with the South Fork Rock Creek and Simms Rd  showing damage.   Debris was reported across Idaho route 37 by a local farmer.   A basement also was reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rain fell with several reports of 1 to 3 inches falling.   Flash flooding was widespread across southeasy Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Evidence of flash flooding across Hess Road northeast of Holbrook with minor residual damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rain fell with several reports of 1 to 3 inches falling.   Flash flooding was widespread across southeasy Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 3 inches of heavy rain fell in much of Fremont County causing extensive flooding.  15 people were stranded east/southeast of Macks Inn along the Henry's fork River between Big Springs and Macks Inn due to the rain.   Many were suffering hypothermia and were treated on scene.  Multiple private roads on the Ashton Hill were washing out.  A running track was flooded at North Fremont High School.   Moving water was filmed across fields and roads in the area of N 3700 E and E 1400 N.  Crops and fields suffered damage in the Marysville area.  Several sections of the Ashton-Tetonia Rail Trail were flooded, washed out and covered in mud.  3 cabins reported flooded north northwest of Squirrel.  The Henrys Fork River rose one half foot between 5:45 Pm MDT and 9 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with wind gusts over 40 mph caused US Highway 20 to close on the 27th as well as Idaho Highway 87.  Many automobile accidents and slide offs were reported on US 20.   Both closed at around 5 PM MST on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with wind gusts over 40 mph caused US Highway 20 to close on the 27th from Ashton to Targhee Pass as well as Idaho Highway 87 between US Highway 20 and Raynolds Pass.  Many automobile accidents and slide offs were reported on US 20.   Seven to 8 crashes were reported on US 20.  Both highways closed at around 5 PM MST on the 27th.  Snow amounts were in the 3 to 6 inch range in Island Park with 10 inches estimated at the White Elephant SNOTEL site.  Wind gusts were widespread in the 35 to 45 mph range.  Roads were reopened at 3 AM MST Monday the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The entirety of November was extremely cold in southeast Idaho.   The 5 official ASOS climatological sites all had top 10 coldest month with 4 of them in the top 5 of coldest Novembers ever.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Challis ASOS mean November temperatures of 21.4 degrees made it the 3rd coldest November recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ross Fork wildfire was reported during the afternoon of August 9th   It was a grass fire that several fire agencies assisted on.  It grew to 6,400 acres and high winds caused it to jump lines.   Residents were evacuated from Ross Fork Creek Road on the 11th.  After several bouts of losing control the fire was contained and only one outbuilding was destroyed.   Several homes were not damaged.   Containment occurred on August 11th by 6 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ross Fork wildfire was reported during the afternoon of August 9th northeast of Pocatello near the intersection of Ross Fork and Ross Fork Creek roads   It was a grass fire that several fire agencies assisted on.  It grew to 6,400 acres and high winds caused it to jump lines.   Residents were evacuated from Ross Fork Creek Road on the 11th.  After several bouts of losing control the fire was contained and only one outbuilding was destroyed.   Several homes were not damaged.   Containment occurred on August 11th by 6 pm.

Strong winds at 67 mph were measured at the airport and it caused local power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat burst knocked out power to the airport and NIFC, along with multiple neighborhoods on the Boise Bench.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure settled over the Colombia River basin bringing hot daytime temperatures and warm overnight lows. The consecutive days of heat made it difficult to cool locations without air conditioning. Daily record highs were set for at least one observation site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highs temperatures reached from 100 to 108 degrees Fahrenheit with overnight lows in the upper 60s to lower 70s. Pierce ID set record high temperatures on July 29 and August 1, and also remained above 95F for 6 straight days from July 29 to August 3.  This was its 2nd longest such streak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought heavy snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grangemont ITD webcam showed heavy snow and snow-covered conditions. Total snow amounts during the event include: 7 inches in 5 hours at the Shanghi Summit and 2.5 inches at Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought heavy snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL recorded heavy snow with 3 inches falling from 1400 to 1500PST. Other snow amounts include: 5.6 inches at Dixie and 8 inches at the Twin Lakes SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought heavy snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall A heavy burst of snow deposited 2 inches of snow in the Grangeville region that melted and froze on the roadways. A weather spotter reported that the vehicles on US-95 and Johnson Cutoff were bumper to bumper for hours because of icy conditions. The Idaho County sheriff issued a road advisory stating that the US-95 was backed up due to multiple slide offs and accidents. They also stated that Harpster Grade was extremely slick and snow-covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought heavy snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall A snow band brought heavy snow that melted and refroze onto US-95 towards Frei Hill and Cottonwood, and created icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought heavy snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall Strong wind gusting to 35 mph with blizzard conditions were reported in Salmon. Observations showed that temperatures dropped from near 40 degrees to the upper 20s with the squall which likely contributed to icy conditions. Estimated total snowfall in Salmon was 1 inch while near 8 inches fell at Williams Creek Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dew point depression of 40 to 50 degrees provided good evaporative cooling potential, allowing for the acceleration of thunderstorm downdrafts. These stronger downdrafts translated into severe outflow flow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RAWS station above the city of Salmon recorded a wind gust to 66 mph. There was extensive damage within Salmon, mainly centered on the southern side of the city. Damage included damage to roofs, RVs blown over, and high school bleachers blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four ASOS sites recorded their hottest September temperatures ever.   They were Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Burley, and Challis.  For the entire month the number of record high temperatures recorded were:  Pocatello 5, Idaho Falls 6, Burley 3, Challis 5, and Stanley 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pocatello ASOS at the airport recorded the hottest September temperatures ever recorded with 102 degrees on the 3rd.   Two other days reached 100 degrees and 5 record highs were recorded during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously high upper level ridge stayed over the region for several days in early September. This led to 2 consecutive days of record high temperatures in the region, with many stations recording triple digit temperatures. There was one recorded fatality from this extreme heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple newspapers reported on an infant that died after being left in a hot car in New Plymouth, Idaho on September 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several occurrences of severe thunderstorm wind gusts and flooding with heavy rain were reported on September 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive street and road flooding occurred on the evening of the 21st in the Pocatello/Chubbuck area as rains in excess of a half inch fell.  The intersection of East Oak St and Yellowstone Ave flooded.  The Center Street Underpass accumulated 18 to 24 inches of water.  Flooding at N 3rd Ave and E Fremont St affected a business.  Hawthorne Rd flooded with deep water at the interstate 86 underpass and was closed from 830 pm to 10 pm MDT.  US Route 30 also closed between Hawthorne Rd and W Cedar St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the slow moving Low continued through the region, another round of thunderstorms over the Eastern Washington produced large hail and strong winds to the region. The storms impacted extreme Eastern WA and ID Panhandle. Thunderstorms developed along an axis of instability stretching along the border between WA and ID.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved Northeast from the Craig Mountain area through Kamiah ID. It brought large hail around 1 inch and wind damage. The wind damage included trees and power lines in the town of Kamiah and along highways 64, 12, and 162. A roof also fell onto a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed Low over the region shifted over North Idaho and increased instability over the area. The instability mixed with afternoon convection to develop isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed West of Kellogg around 1135PST and moved East into Montana around 1300PST. Hail size ranged from half an inch to one inch. Estimated one inch hail size fell around 1230 near Wallace. Hail covered interstate 90 east of Wallace during the storm. Time estimated from radar. There was also wind damage. Utility Company reported a tree and wires down near Big Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds developed in Grangeville and vicinity as an area of low pressure moved into northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP station a few miles northwest of Grangeville measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph. The Grangeville Idaho County Airport recorded a wind gust of 61 mph. No significant power outages were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture brought multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel across the Clearwater Mountains. South wind gusts up to 50 mph developed ahead of a cold front in Grangeville and vicinity. Heavy snow briefly developed in Grangeville and vicinity as the cold front passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph led to areas of blowing snow in Grangeville and vicinity. Two hours of heavy snow was observed in Grangeville, with 4 inches of snow being reported. Other sites picked up 1 to 3 inches of accumulation, including Orofino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Suspected lightning started the Columbus Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbus Fire was detected on August 22nd, 2022. It is located approximately 6 miles northeast of Murray, ID, and is within a mile of the Idaho/Montana state line. This fire was likely a holdover lightning start from the same storm that started the Bear Gulch Fire. It has burned 2298 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ross Fark wildfire began in Camas County August 14th and spread into Blaine and Custer Counties in late August into early September.   It was timber litter, timber and short grass that fueled the fire.  By September 4th the fire expanded from 3 thousand acres to over 5 thousand acres.  Blaine County Sherriff's office issued a mandatory evacuation notice on September 4th for the Sawtooth City, Smiley Creek, Lake Alturas, and the Beaver Creek areas in the Stanley Basin all cabins and campgrounds.   State Highway 75 was shut down 5 miles south and north of Smiley Creek.  A disaster declaration was declared on September 6th by Blaine County.  The fire had grown to 14 thousand acres by September 6th.  Local engines from Ketchum, Sun Valley, Hailey, Wood River Fire and Rescue and Bellevue were assigned to protect buildings around Smiley Creek and Alturas Lake. on September 5th.   By the 7th the fire had run to and across highway 75.  By the 7th   Type 2 team was assigned.  Two structures were burned down.  By September 7th the following access to lakes and drainages were closed:  Pettit Lake and Trailhead, Yellowbelly Lake, Pole Creek, Upper Salmon River Headwaters, Frenchman Creek, and Alturas Lake.  Air quality became very unhealthy in Smiley Creek, Haily and Ketchum due to the smoke from the fire.  FEMA authorized federal funds to help with firefighting costs which neared 6 million dollars.  The fire grew to over 31 thousand acres by the 8th with much of the fire growth caused by high winds.  Highway 75 remained closed between mile markers 146 and 172.  By September10th the fire caused Galena Lodge to be closed for the season due to the fire as it reached over 35 thousand acres.  By mid September the fire reached close to 38 thousand acres and stopped growing and was 64 percent contained by the end of the month and closures were greatly reduced.

It was timber litter, timber and short grass that fueled the fire.  By September 4th the fire expanded from 3 thousand acres to over 5 thousand acres.  Blaine County Sherriff's office issued a mandatory evacuation notice on September 4th for the Sawtooth City, Smiley Creek, Lake Alturas, and the Beaver Creek areas in the Stanley Basin all cabins and campgrounds.   State Highway 75 was shut down 5 miles south and north of Smiley Creek.  A disaster declaration was declared on September 6th by Blaine County.  The fire had grown to 14 thousand acres by September 6th.  Local engines from Ketchum, Sun Valley, Hailey, Wood River Fire and Rescue and Bellevue were assigned to protect buildings around Smiley Creek and Alturas Lake. on September 5th.   By the 7th the fire had run to and across highway 75.  By the 7th   Type 2 team was assigned.  Two structures were burned down.  By September 7th the following access to lakes and drainages were closed:  Pettit Lake and Trailhead, Yellowbelly Lake, Pole Creek, Upper Salmon River Headwaters, Frenchman Creek, and Alturas Lake.  Air quality became very unhealthy in Smiley Creek, Haily and Ketchum due to the smoke from the fire.  FEMA authorized federal funds to help with firefighting costs which neared 6 million dollars.  The fire grew to over 31 thousand acres by the 8th with much of the fire growth caused by high winds.  Highway 75 remained closed between mile markers 146 and 172.  By September10th the fire caused Galena Lodge to be closed for the season due to the fire as it reached over 35 thousand acres.  By mid September the fire reached close to 38 thousand acres and stopped growing and was 64 percent contained by the end of the month and closures were greatly reduced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire ignited north of Chubbuck at 1:45 MDT September 8th on BLM land.  It spread to the Fort Hall Indian Reservation and private land.  Some structures were threatened and led to some evacuations on the reservation.  Portions of Ross Fork Road and 2 and a half mile road were closed on Thursday the 8th.   The fire reached 8,700 acres and was fully contained on September 11th.  No structures ended up damaged and no injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low moved through the Pacific Northwest at the end of November. With below normal temperatures of single digits and teens and ample moisture, it brought widespread snow. The heaviest snow fell in the North Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 14.5 inches of snow near Hayden Idaho. The communities around Lake Coeur D'Alene reported snow amounts ranging from 5 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Bear Gulch Wildfire on August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Gulch Fire was lightning caused and detected on August 18th, 2022.  It continued through the month of September. It is located approximately 6 miles east of Murray, ID, and is within a mile of the Idaho/Montana state line. It has burned 100 acres of timber and brush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Caledonia Wildfire on September 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caledonia Fire is approximately 18 miles Northeast of Kelly Forks Work Center. It burned 1535 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low moved through the Pacific Northwest at the end of November. With below normal temperatures of single digits and teens and ample moisture, it brought widespread snow. The heaviest snow fell in the North Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 9.5 inches for Nov 29-30 near Naples Idaho. There were heavy snow reports throughout North Idaho. Reports of 6 to 14 inches for Priest River, Sandpoint, Athol, Spirit Lake, Blanchard, and Rathdrum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Low moved through the Pacific Northwest at the end of November. With below normal temperatures of single digits and teens and ample moisture, it brought widespread snow. The heaviest snow fell in the North Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Kellogg Idaho reported 7.4 inches of snow. A range of 3 to 6 inches was spread out from Lotus and Cataldo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Suspected lightning started the Columbus Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbus Fire was detected on August 22 and active through September. It is located approximately 6 miles northeast of Murray, ID, and is within a mile of the Idaho/Montana state line. This fire was likely a holdover lightning start from the same storm that started the Bear Gulch Fire. It has burned 2298 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the fire on August 24. It became the Kootenai Complex on September 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russell Mountain Fire was reported early afternoon on August 24, 2022 and remained active through September. It is located within the Selkirk Mountain Range on the Bonners Ferry Ranger District of the Idaho Panhandle National Forests. It is approximately 10 miles southwest of Copeland Idaho. It has burned 4811 acres of timber and brush. The Kootenai River Complex consists of the Eneas Peak, Katka, Russell Mountain, Scotch Creek, and Trout Fires. They have combined to burn 25401 acres. Cost of the Complex is around 15 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely strong Pacific cold front brought wind gusts in the 50 to 80 mph range with localized blizzard conditions in the mountains and then extremely cold wind chills followed behind an arctic cold frontal passage.   Snow amounts in the mountains were generally light and mainly in the 1 to 5 inch range but the combination with the wind brought many road closures to extreme eastern Idaho on the 21st into the 22nd.  Some school closures occurred on the 21st and 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme winds caused widespread blowing and drifting snow with extensive road closures.  US Highway 20 was closed from Ashton to the Montana state line.   State Highway 87 was closed from US 20 to the Montana state line.   Fremont County School District 215 was closed on the 21st due to the road conditions.  Wind chill readings of -50 to -60 were recorded on the 22nd and 23d after the arctic frontal passage.

Some school closures occurred on the 21st and 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely strong winds gusting 60 to 70 mph at times brought widespread blowing and drifting snow.  US Highway 20 closed from Ashton to the Montana State Line.  State Highway 33 closed from Newdale to Tetonia and State Highway 32 closed from Tetonia to Ashton.   Fremont County School District 215 closed due to road conditions on the 21st.  Extreme cold wind chills of -35 to -45 were recorded on the 22nd behind the system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system started to impact the region on November 4 and lasted through November 7. A cold front pushed across the region on Nov 4 bringing light snow and strong winds. The winds generally ranged from the 30s to 40s MPH. A few locations received gusts into the 50s. It knocked down trees and caused power outages. A local business was damaged in Post Falls.  The following Arctic air brought the first significant snow fall for the region through Nov 7. Snow amounts across the region were 4 to 10 inches for the through the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 7.8 inches South of St. Maries.
